methods: METHOD DETAILS Gene targeting OTX2-dTAG mESCs were generated using CRISPR/Cas9. Homology arms covering 778bp upstream and 680bp downstream of the Otx2 C-terminus were PCR-amplified from genomic DNA and cloned with the FKBP12 F36V -2xHA-P2A-NLS-EGFP construct into pBluescript (Stratagene) using Gibson assembly. The OTX2-FKBP12 F36V -2xHA-P2A-NLS-EGFP targeting vector was transfected into the abovementioned ESC lines along with pX330-puro R or blasticidin R vector harboring guide RNAs targeting the Otx2 C-terminus. 2 x 10 5 ESCs were cultured overnight and transfected with 3μg of each plasmid using TransIT-293 (Mirus Bio 2700). Cells were replated at low density onto 10cm dishes with the corresponding antibiotic selection for 24–72 hours. Individual clones were screened by PCR followed by TOPO cloning (Invitrogen) and sequencing. Mouse ESC culture and epiblast conversion ESCs were cultured in KO DMEM (Gibco 10829018) supplemented with 15% FBS (Gemini Benchmark), 2mM Glutamax (Gibco 35050079), 0.1mM nonessential amino acids (Gibco 11140076), 0.1mM 2-mercaptoethanol (Gibco 21985023), 1000U/ml leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF; in house), and 100μg/ml penicillin/streptomycin (Gibco 15140163) and maintained on a feeder layer of mitomycin C-treated mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs) on gelatin-coated plates. For ESC to EpiLC conversion, ESCs were lifted using collagenase IV (Thermo Fisher 17104019), centrifuged for 4min at 120g, and dissociated with Accutase (Thermo Fisher 00455556). 15,000 cells/cm 2 were seeded onto fibronectin (Millipore FC010; 16.7ug/ml)-coated plates in N2B27 media with 12.5ng/ml heat stable bFGF (Thermo PHG0360) and 20ng/ml Activin A (Peprotech 120–14P) (FA media) supplemented with 1% Knockout Serum Replacement (Gibco 10828010). EpiLCs were harvested or converted to EpiSCs after 48hrs differentiation. For EpiSC conversion EpiLCs were dissociated with TrypLE (Thermo Fisher 12605010) and plated onto gelatin-coated plates with feeders at 15,000 cells/cm 2 in EpiSC media (FA supplemented with 175nM NVP-TNKS656 (Selleck S7238). Mouse definitive endoderm, gastric foregut and paraxial mesoderm specification EpiSCs cultured between passages 2–7 were differentiated first to primitive streak/mesendodermal progenitors (PS) and then to definitive endoderm (DE) as described 33 . Briefly, EpiSCs were seeded onto 96, 48, or 24well plates coated with 10ug/ml Laminin 521 (StemCell Technologies 77003) in plating media. Plating media (chemically defined media, CDM) is 1:1 IMDM (Gibco 12440053) and DMEM/F12 with Glutamax (Gibco 10565018), 1% chemically defined lipid concentrate (Thermo Fisher 11905031), monothioglycerol (Sigma M6145), Apotransferrin (R&D 3188AT), 0.7 μg/ml insulin (Millipore Sigma I0516), and polyvinyl alcohol (Sigma P8136) supplemented with 12.5ng/ml bFGF, 20ng/ml activin A, 175nM NVP, 1% KSR and 2 μM thiazovivin (Millipore Sigma SML1045). PS was induced 5–6 hours after seeding with CDM + 40ng/ml activin A and 3μM CHIR99021 (Biovision 16775). DE was induced 16 hours after PS with CDM + 100ng/ml activin A and 100nM LDN193189 (Reprocell 04–0074). Cells were washed 1x in PBS −/− between all media changes. To convert DE to antral gastric progenitors (posterior foregut), DE cultured for 48 hours was grown for an additional 48 hours in CDM with 100nM LDN193189 and 2% FBS. 2μM retinoic acid was added, and cells cultured for 24 hours before fixation and staining. Cells were treated with 500nM dTAG13 (Tocris 6605) in DMSO or vehicle control in culture media at the indicated amounts of time. PM was induced from PS by culture in CMD supplemented with 6 μM CHIR99021 for 40–48 hours. Micropatterns ESCs were converted to EpiLCs for 48hrs and seeded onto micropattern chips (CYTOO Arena A) as described 31 . Briefly, 2–6 x 10 6 EpiLCs were plated in EpiLC media onto micropattern chips coated with 10μg/ml Laminin 521. Media was replaced with FA after 2h and cells allowed to aggregate on micropatterns for 24hrs. 200 ng/ml Wnt3a (R&D 5036-WNP) was added to induce anterior differentiation. Chips were fixed and stained 48hrs after Wnt3a addition. Human ESC culture and genetic engineering DE conversion Human MEL-1 and H9 ESCs were cultured on tissue culture plates coated with vitronectin (5 μg/ml) in Essential 8 Flex media with supplements and normocin (50 μg/ml) (“E8”). Cells were passaged with home-made EDTA Dissociation Solution (0.5 mM EDTA and 35 mM NaCl in PBS) during maintenance or with Accutase for freezing or when preparing single cell suspensions. Cells were frozen in Stem Cell Banker. After thawing, Y-27632 was added at 10 μM to the culture media for 24 hours. A targeting vector for generation of the human OTX2-dTAG allele was built by combining a 1252bp PCR amplicon (chr14:56,801,072–56,802,323 in hg38) generated from MEL-1 genomic DNA with a FKBPV-2xHA-P2A-NLS-EGFP cassette, using Gibson assembly. The resulting vector was introduced into MEL-1 and H9 hESCs together with the pX330 Cas9 vector expression a guide RNA (5’-GTACAGGTCTTCACAAAACC-3’) targeting the OTX2 STOP codon using lipofection in mTeSR media mixed with CloneR solution (1:10). Transfected cells were selected for 48h with G418 (500 μg/ml), using mTeSR:CloneR (1:10). After selection, cells were seeded at clonal density and grown in E8 supplemented with CloneR (1:10) for three days and another five days in E8 without CloneR when colonies with undifferentiated morphology and homogenous nuclear EGFP fluorescence were picked. Clonal lines were expanded and characterized by flow cytometry (intensity and homogeneity of EGFP fluorescence), genotyping PCR (using the primers 5’-GGCAGGGGAAATTGTGTGTT-3’ and 5’-TCGTAAAGTTTCAGTGCAGCT-3’ which localize on either side outside of the OTX2 homology region included in the targeting vector), by Sanger sequencing of the PCR amplicon and by IF using antibodies against HA and OTX2. Confirmed homozygous lines were karyotyped before use in differentiation experiments. Human DE specification Single cell suspensions of hESCs were seeded in E8 supplemented with 10 μM Y-27632 onto cell culture vessel that were coated with Laminin-521 at 4°C for 24h to achieve a seeding density of 30–40%. The next day, cells were washed with prewarmed F12 based media and cultured for 24h in PS induction media. Cells were then washed with prewarmed F12 based media and cultured for another 40h to 48h in DE induction media, with one media change after 24h of culture. Two protocols slightly differing in their use of signaling modulators were used. Protocol 1 uses the same PS and DE induction media applied to mouse DE. Protocol 2 uses PS induction media with 100 ng/ml Activin A, 2 μM CHIR99021 and 100 mM PIK90 (Selleckchem S1187) and DE induction media with 250 nM LDN1931189 (see also Fig. S5C ). Both protocols use identical base media and overall experimental timing. Immunofluorescence For micropatterns, chips were washed 2x in PBS −/−, fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde (Thermo Fisher 28908) for 15min, and washed again 2x in PBS −/−. Chips were stained as described. Micropattern chips were mounted on microscope slides with Fluoromount G (Thermo Fisher 00495802) and imaged on an LSM880 confocal microscope (Zeiss). For EpiSCs, DE and foregut, cells were washed and fixed in wells as above, blocked in PBS +/+ with 1% BSA, 0.1% Triton-X-100, and 3% donkey serum (Sigma D9663) and stained in primary antibodies overnight. Cells were washed 3x in PBS +/+ with 0.1% Triton X-100 (PBS-T) and then incubated in secondary antibodies for 1 hour. Cells were then again washed 3x in PBS-T. Hoechst33342 (Thermo Fisher H3570) was added to the last wash for 15 minutes for nuclear staining. Images were taken on an EVOS Cell Imaging System (Thermo Fisher) or Leica DMi8 microscope. Primary antibodies used were anti-SOX17 (R&D AF1924), anti-FOXA2 (Abcam 108422), anti-T (R&D AF2085), anti-HA (Abcam 9110) anti-OCT4 (Santa Cruz 8628), anti-GFP (Aves GFP1020), anti-SOX2 (Invitrogen 14981180), anti-PDX1 (Abcam 47308). For both micropatterns and directed differentiation, primary antibodies were used at 1:300 except anti-HA and anti-GFP (1:1000) and anti-PDX1 (1:100). Secondary antibodies (Alexa Fluor, Life Technologies, and Jackson Immunoresearch) were used at 1:500. Flow cytometry Cells were washed with PBS −/−, dissociated with Accutase, resuspended in FACS buffer (80% PBS −/−, 20% FBS, 0.1% 0.5M EDTA), and incubated with indicated antibodies for 15min at room temperature in the dark. Cells were washed once in PBS −/−, resuspended in FACS buffer with DAPI, and analyzed on a BD FACS Canto. Data were analyzed using FlowJo. CXCR4 antibody conjugated to APC (eBioscience 17999182) and PDGFRa antibody conjugated to PE-Cy7 (eBioscience 25140180) were used at 1:200 dilution. DLL1 (Biolegend 128307) and CD117 (Thermo 12–1179-42) antibodies conjugated to PE were used at 1:50. RT-qPCR Total RNA was extracted using TRIzol (Life Technologies 15596018) and purified with the RNA Clean and Concentrator kit (Zymo R1014). 1μg of RNA/sample was used for reverse transcription using the iScript kit (BioRad 1708841). qPCR was performed on cDNA samples in triplicate using PowerUp SYBR green PCR master mix (Thermo Fisher A25778) on an Applied Biosystems QuantStudio3. Primers used are in Table S6 . Western blotting Cells were washed with PBS −/− and harvested with Trypsin (Life Technologies 25200114). Nuclear lysates were prepared using the NE-PER kit (Thermo Fisher 78835) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Western blots were performed using the following antibodies: anti-HA (Abcam 9110), anti-OTX2 (Abcam 21990), anti-histone H3 (Abcam 1791). Protein concentration was measured using Bradford Reagent (BioRad 5000201) and samples were boiled, resuspended in Laemmli Sample Buffer (BioRad), and run using BioRad PROTEAN System or Invitrogen precast gels. scRNA-seq Cells at indicated stages were dissociated using Accutase to single-cell suspension in PBS−/− with 0.04% BSA. Samples were labeled with Cell Multiplexing Oligos (10x Genomics Chromium Next GEM Single Cell 3ʹ Reagent Kit v3.1 Dual Index) and live cells sorted using DAPI on a BD Influx Cell Sorter. Samples were then pooled for multiplexing in PBS−/− with 0.04% BSA (targeting 10,000 cells/sample) and single-cell libraries generated according to the manufacturer's instructions (10x Genomics Chromium Single Cell Gene Expression - Cell Multiplexing). All cell sorts and deep sequencing were performed at the Weill Cornell Flow Cytometry and Weill Cornell Genomics Resources Core Facilities respectively. RNA-seq EpiSCs were cultured and PS and DE induced for 21hrs in the presence or absence of dTAG13. Total RNA from three subclone replicates was prepared by TRIzol and chloroform extraction and purified using the Zymo RNA Clean & Concentrator kit according to the manufacturer’s instructions. mRNA libraries (polyA-enriched) and sequencing (paired-end 150, NovaSeq X plus) were prepared and performed by Novogene. CUT&RUN CUT&RUN on OTX2-HA was performed on replicates of 500,000 EpiSCs, PS or DE cells, and similar as previously described 61 . For DE, live cells were selected by DAPI sort on an Influx Cell Sorter before performing CUT&RUN. BioMag Plus Concanavalin A beads (ConA beads, PolySciences NC1358578) were washed twice in 100μl cold Bead Activation Buffer (20mM HEPES pH7.5, 10mM KCl, 1mM CaCl 2 , 1mM MnCl 2 ). 20μg of ConA beads per CUT&RUN reaction were used. Sorted cells were washed twice in Wash Buffer (20mM HEPES pH7.5, 150mM NaCl, 0.5mM Spermidine (Acros AC132740050), ½ Protease Inhibitor tablet) and bound to activated ConA beads in Wash Buffer for 10 minutes at room temperature. Samples were incubated in PCR strip tubes with 1:100 anti-HA (CST C29F4 #3724) or anti-IgG (EpiCypher 130042) in Antibody Buffer (Wash Buffer with 2mM EDTA and 0.01% Digitonin (Millipore Sigma 300410)) overnight with gentle rocking at 4°C. Samples were washed twice in Digitonin Buffer (Washer Buffer with 0.01% Digitonin) and ProteinA/G MNase (EpiCypher 15–1016) was bound to samples for 1 hour at 4°C. ProteinA/G MNase was diluted at 20x in 50μl Antibody Buffer according to manufacturer’s instructions. Samples were washed twice in Digitonin Buffer again and MNase digestion was activated with 1μl 100mM CaCl 2 in 50μl Digitonin Buffer for 2 hours. To extract bound chromatin fragments digestion was quenched using 33μl STOP Buffer (340mM NaCl, 20mM EDTA, 4mM EGTA, 50μg/ml RNAse A, 50μg/ml glycogen, 0.015 ng/μl E. coli spike-in) and samples incubated at 37°C for 20 minutes. Chromatin was separated from ConA beads by centrifuging 5 minutes at 16,000g and saving supernatant. DNA was purified by adding 0.1% SDS and 5μg Proteinase K for 10 min at 70°C, performing phenol-chloroform extraction, and precipitation in 100% ethanol at −80°C overnight. Pellets were then washed in 100% ethanol and resuspended in 12μl nuclease free water. CUT&RUN libraries were prepared using the Thruplex Library Prep kit (Takara R400676) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The number of cycles for library PCR amplification was determined by running 10% of each library sample in an Applied Biosystems qPCR machine with 0.25μl Eva Green dye (Biotium 31000) for 40 cycles and adding cycles to each sample as described 62 . Libraries were size selected with 1.5x volume SPRI beads (Beckman Coulter B23317 ) and sequenced at the Weill Cornell Genomics Resources Core Facility on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 (PE-50, 30 million reads per sample). CUT&Tag CUT&Tag was performed as previously described 45 on 100,000 EpiSCs, PS or DE cells for H3K27ac (EpiCypher 13–0059) and H3K4me1 (abcam 8895). ConA beads were washed and activated as described for CUT&RUN. Cells were harvested with Accutase, fixed for 1 minute in 0.1% formaldehyde and quenched in 125mM glycine. Samples were washed once in PBS −/− and incubated in Nuclear Extraction (NE) Buffer (20 mM HEPES–KOH, pH 7.5, 10 mM KCl, 0.1% Triton X-100, 20% Glycerol, 0.5 mM Spermidine, protease inhibitors) for 10 minutes. Samples were then washed once in NE Buffer and incubated overnight at 4°C in PCR strip tubes with 0.5ug of anti-H3K27ac or anti-H3K4me1 in CUT&RUN Antibody Buffer with gentle rocking. Samples were incubated for 30 minutes with 0.5ug anti-rabbit Secondary Antibody (EpiCypher 13–1047) in Antibody Buffer and washed twice. Samples were then incubated for 1 hour in Protein A/G Tn5 (EpiCypher 15–1017) and Digitonin300 Buffer (20 mM HEPES, pH 7.5, 300 mM NaCl, 0.5 mM Spermidine, protease inhibitors, 0.01% Digitonin). 2.5ul pA/G-Tn5 was diluted in 50ul Digitonin300 Buffer per reaction. Samples were washed twice in Digitonin300 Buffer and chromatin was tagmented for 1hr at 37°C in 50ul Tagmentation Buffer (20 mM HEPES, pH 7.5, 300 mM NaCl, 0.5 mM Spermidine, protease inhibitors, 0.01% Digitonin, 10 mM MgCl 2 ). Tagmentation was quenched in TAPS Buffer (10 mM TAPS, pH 8.5, 0.2 mM EDTA). Chromatin fragments were released for 1hr at 58°C in SDS Release Buffer (10 mM TAPS, pH 8.5, 0.1% SDS) and neutralized in 0.67% Triton-X 100. Libraries were amplified using the Illumina i5 universal and i7 primers and CUTANA Non-Hot Start 2X PCR Master Mix (EpiCypher 15–1018) with 14–19 cycles of PCR according to instructions in the EpiCypher CUTANA Direct-to-PCR CUT&Tag protocol. Libraries were size selected with 1.5x volume SPRI beads (Beckman Coulter B23317 ) and sequenced at the Weill Cornell Genomics Resources Core Facility on an Illumina NovaSeq X Plus (PE-50, 15–30 million reads per sample). ATAC-seq DE cultured with dTAG13 or DMSO vehicle for 24 hours was sorted for live cells on Influx as described for CUT&RUN. ATAC-seq was performed on three technical replicates of 50,000 cells each for DMSO or dTAG13-treated cultures as described 63 using the Nextera DNA library preparation kit (Illumina FC121103 ) and NEBNext High-Fidelity 2X PCR Master Mix (NEB M0541). Libraries were sequenced on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 (PE-50, 20 million reads per sample).
results: Results Identification of candidate regulators of germ layer specification We analyzed genomics and phenotypic datasets to identify candidate transcriptional regulators of germ layer specification among a list of 1,682 murine TFs 25 . We set as criteria I) strong upregulation – fold change (FC)>5 and adjusted p-value (padj) <0.05 – during the transition of mESCs to epiblast-like stem cells (EpiLCs) resembling the post-implantation epiblast 26 ( Table S1 ); II) elevated expression throughout formation and patterning of at least one germ layer in the mouse embryo based on single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) 5 , 6 and III) developmental organ phenotypes in KO mice 27 . This approach identified seven candidates ( Fig.S1A ), among which, Otx2 – encoding a highly conserved homeobox TF 19 , 21 – demonstrated the most significant upregulation during the transition from naïve to primed pluripotency ( Table S1 ). While the importance of OTX2 for development of the anterior visceral endoderm and anteriorization of the neuroectoderm (NE) are well-documented 20 , 28 , we also observed high Otx2 expression along the trajectory of primary gut tube (“gut tube” from hereon) development ( epiblast → anterior PS → DE → anterior gut tube) ( Fig.1A , B and Fig. S1B ). To study OTX2 functions, we generated mESCs homozygous for a “multipurpose” transgenic cassette at the Otx2 STOP codon. In addition to a dTAG 13 for controlled OTX2 degradation, the cassette contains a double HA tag for immunoprecipitation (IP) and immunofluorescence (IF), and a transcriptional reporter encoding a nuclear-localized EGFP ( Fig.1C ). Flow cytometric analysis revealed a low Otx2 expression in mESCs, but significant upregulation in derivative EpiLCs and epiblast stem cells (EpiSCs) ( Fig.S1C ), an observation consistent with previous results 29 and Otx2 expression in vivo 30 . Treatment of OTX2-dTAG EpiLCs with the inducer molecule dTAG-13 resulted in efficient (>90%) degradation of OTX2 within one hour (h) of culture ( Fig.1D ). OTX2 depletion impairs DE formation from mouse pluripotent stem cells We converted OTX2-dTAG EpiLCs into anterior PS derivatives that emerge during gastrulation using micropattern technology 31 ( Fig.S1D ). Immunostaining revealed a quantitative reduction in cells expressing the DE marker SOX17 31 , 32 after depletion of OTX2 at the onset of differentiation, while FOXA2 + SOX17 − putative axial mesoderm cells were still efficiently formed ( Fig.1E , F ). We also observed a slight increase in the levels of the epiblast marker SOX2 after loss of OTX2 ( Fig.1E , F ). These observations support a previously unappreciated and specific role of OTX2 for DE development. To dissect the functions of OTX2 during DE development, we utilized a directed differentiation approach that in a synchronized manner converts OTX2-dTAG EpiSCs into PS followed by DE 33 ( Fig.1G and Fig.S1E ). Otx2 expression (measured by Otx2-EGFP or HA IF) was detected in >95% of cells at all stages and gradually increased in intensity with the highest levels attained in DE ( Fig.1H , Fig.S1F ). OTX2 protein levels were reduced to background after 1h of culture in the presence of dTAG-13 ( Fig.S1G ). OTX2 depletion at the PS stage ( Fig.1G ) resulted in reduced differentiation into DE (CXCR4 + PDGFRA − cells) and an increase in cells expressing the mesoderm-associated surface marker PDGFRA 34 ( Fig.1I , Fig.S1H ). In addition, the levels of SOX17 were reduced ( Fig.1J , Fig.S1I ). These effects were specific, as dTAG-13 treatment did not affect SOX17 in parental (WT) cells ( Fig.S1J ), and differentiation was unaffected by DMSO treatment of OTX2-dTAG cells ( Fig.S1H ). OTX2 works in a partially cell-non-autonomous manner during anterior neuroectoderm induction 18 . However, mixing experiments with OTX2-dTAG and parental (WT) EpiSCs revealed no evidence for rescue of CXCR4 levels in OTX2-depleted cells by WT cells or reduced CXCR4 levels in WT cells in the presence of OTX2-depleted cells ( Fig.S1K ). These observations support cell-autonomous functions of OTX2 in driving DE-associated gene expression. OTX2 loss in PS affects specific transcriptional programs in DE We applied scRNA-seq to characterize transcriptional consequences of OTX2 depletion during the directed differentiation of EpiSCs into DE. Control DE and DE depleted for OTX2 from PS onwards (OTX2 depl_PS DE) showed extinction of pluripotency-associated transcripts, absence of markers for visceral ( Sox7 ) and primitive ( Ttr ) endoderm, and no differences in cell cycle state based on the expression of the G2/M indicators Aurka and Plk1 35 ( Fig.S2A ). OTX2 depl_PS DE also expressed high levels of the DE-associated markers CD24a and Hhex 6 , 36 , 37 ( Fig.S2A ). Nevertheless, UMAP projection showed that OTX2 depl_PS DE formed a distinct cluster ( Fig.2A ). Accordingly, we identified a total of 1,646 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) (RNA score>1; log2FC>1; padj<0.05) ( Table S2 ), with similar fractions of downregulated (44.9%) and upregulated (55.1%) genes ( Fig.2B ), indicating profound changes to DE identity. At the gene level, DE-associated TFs such as Sox17, Isl1, and Rfx6 were downregulated upon OTX2 depletion, while TFs such as Prdm6 , Mixl1, and Tfap2c associated with other embryonic lineages emerging in post-gastrulation embryos, including early mesoderm, neuroectoderm, and primordial germ cells (PGCs) were upregulated ( Fig.2B and Fig.S2B ). We also observed slightly elevated levels of Eomes and Foxa2 ( Fig.S2A ), TFs required for the formation of DE and mesodermal populations 38 , 39 , and upregulation of mesoderm-associated Pdgfra ( Fig.S2C ). Ectopic lineage markers were largely co-expressed in the same cells in OTX2 depl_PS DE cultures ( Fig.S2C ). GO analysis revealed – in addition to dysregulation of endoderm and differentiation pathways – a strong enrichment for genes involved in WNT signaling and cellular adhesion/migration ( Fig.2C ). Specifically, we observed that antagonists of canonical WNT signaling ( Dkk1 , Dkkl1, Sfrp5, Ror2 ) were downregulated, while agonist ( Wnt2 , Wnt3 , Rspo3 , Sulf2 ) and targets ( Axin2 , Lgr5 ) of this pathway were upregulated in absence of OTX2 ( Fig.2D ). Regulators of other signaling pathways involved in gastrulation ( Tgfb2 , Bmp4 ) and of cell migration/adhesion ( Sema6d , Cdh2 , Adamts18 ) were also altered in their expression ( Fig.2D ). We confirmed the dysregulation of key genes upon OTX2 depletion in an independent cell line ( Fig.S2D ). These observations demonstrate that loss of OTX2 during DE specification results in specific gene expression abnormalities. To determine how OTX2 loss at PS redirects DE identity, we extracted the expression kinetics of the major gene groups affected by OTX2 depletion from our scRNA-seq data. Almost all DEGs fell into three categories defined by their behavior in the absence of OTX2: 1) Genes that are normally upregulated during the transition from PS to DE but fail to do so (DE-associated TFs and WNT antagonists), 2) genes that are normally downregulated during this transition but remain expressed (PS-associated TFs and adhesion molecules) and 3) genes that are lowly expressed during the developmental progression from epiblast to DE but become aberrantly upregulated (TFs associated with other lineages and WNT target genes) ( Fig.2E and Table S2 ). This observation supports the notion that OTX2 is required to faithfully execute the signaling-induced transition from PS to DE. We modified a protocol for the differentiation of EpiSCs into paraxial mesoderm (PM) 40 to test whether OTX2 depletion affects other PS-derived lineages. PM ingresses later than DE but is, like DE, derived from anterior parts of the PS 41 . Accordingly, identical signal cues can induce PS capable of generating DE ( Fig.1G ) and PM ( Fig.S3A ). Analysis of surface marker expression 42 , 43 showed that DLL1 + PDGFRA + PM still formed after OTX2 depletion at PS. In fact, the frequency of DLL1 + PDGFRA + cells was elevated in dTAG cultures ( Fig.S3B , C ). Analysis by scRNA-seq revealed two major cell populations: PM1 (characterized by the PS/immature PM markers T and Fgf8 ) and PM2 (characterized by the mature PM markers Tbx6 and Dll1 ) ( Fig.S3D , E ). OTX2-depleted cells contributed to both these populations, but their relative abundance was greater in PM2 ( Fig.S3F ). This moderate shift in the proportion of PM states is in stark contrast with the strong DE phenotype caused by OTX2 loss ( Fig.S3D , F ). Together, these findings suggest that OTX2 is not required to establish PM from PS. OTX2 remains required after DE specification and primes anterior gut programs To compare the molecular consequences of OTX2 loss before and after endodermal identity has been established, we depleted OTX2 24h after DE specification from PS, followed by scRNA-seq. Depletion in DE affected a similar number of genes in DE (775 DEG DOWN and 808 DEG UP ) than depletion at PS ( Fig.S3G and Table S2 ) with overall concordant gene expression changes ( Fig.3A ). Hallmark molecular alterations observed in DE upon OTX2 loss at PS – such as the dysregulation of canonical WNT signaling and cell adhesion/migration regulators and reduced levels of several DE markers – were also observed in OTX2 depl_DE DE ( Fig.3A ). However, upregulation of genes associated with the PS and nascent mesodermal lineages such as Pdgfra, Prdm6 , Eomes , and T was more pronounced in OTX2 depl_PS DE ( Fig.3A ), suggesting a repressive role of OTX2 at these loci may no longer be required once DE has been specified. Gene expression changes caused by OTX2 depletion such as altered WNT and FGF/ERK signaling 10 are consistent with loss or perturbation of anterior-posterior (AP) identity. Indeed, evaluation of a panel of marker genes for emerging endodermal lineages along the AP axis 6 revealed a pronounced failure of OTX2-depleted DE to express anterior gut tube markers ( Fig.3B ). Accordingly, further differentiation of OTX2-depleted DE towards the foregut lineage ( Fig.3C ) revealed an impairment to form clusters of SOX2 + PDX1 + gastric progenitors 33 ( Fig.3D and Fig.S3H ). Together, our analyses document that OTX2 contributes to establishing and maintaining gene expression programs required for successful anteriorization of the developing gut. OTX2 genome occupancy undergoes dynamic changes during DE specification To determine direct OTX2 targets, we conducted CUT&RUN experiments in EpiSCs, PS, and DE, targeting the HA epitope in our degron allele ( Fig.1C ). We identified 45,534 high-confidence OTX2 binding sites, the vast majority in DE (42,566 or 93.5%)( Fig.4A and Table S3 ). CUT&RUN in wildtype cells (i.e., without the HA epitope) revealed only background signal ( Fig.S4A ). While the majority (31,444 or 73.9%) of DE peaks were not present in EpiSCs or PS (“de novo” peaks), significant subsets of peaks were detectable either from the EpiSC (“primed 1”; 4,965 or 11.7%) or the PS (“primed 2”; 6,157 or 14.5%) stage onwards ( Fig.4A ). The drastic increase in OTX2 binding sites – and the elevated strength of primed peaks from EpiSCs to DE ( Fig.S4B ) – coincides with the upregulation of Otx2 during DE specification ( Fig.1H , S1F ). OTX2 binding occurred at sites enriched for motifs of developmental TFs such as SOX2 (primed 1), EOMES (primed 2), and FOXA1 (de novo)( Fig.4B and Table S3 ). Most primed 2 and de novo DE peaks localized to promoter-distal sites ( Fig.S4C ) and enriched for genes associated with endodermal cell fate determination and digestive tract morphogenesis ( Fig.4C ). Primed 1 peaks were enriched for genes associated with gastrulation and mesendoderm development ( Fig.4C ). We also observed OTX2 binding sites specific to EpiSCs (n=958) or PS (n=1,634) or found in both EpiSCs and PS (n=379) ( Fig.4A ), which were enriched for motifs of pluripotency- and gastrulation-associated TFs (OCT4, SOX2, GSC, EOMES) ( Fig.S4D ). Among known OTX2 targets, we observed binding to the epiblast marker Fgf5 29 and Pou5f1 , a key pluripotency-associated locus 24 ( Fig.S4E ). These observations document that OTX2 occupies candidate regulatory elements of critical lineage-associated genes in a stage-specific manner. We observed well-defined OTX2 peaks of all three categories (primed 1, primed 2, de novo) at many DEG DOWN and DEG UP (defined after depletion at PS) ( Figs.4D , E , and Table S3 ). To systematically investigate the relationship between OTX2 binding and the transcriptional responses triggered by its loss, we analyzed the frequency, strength, genomic positioning, and developmental dynamics of OTX2 peaks at high-confidence (RNA score>10) DEGs, covering 448 DEG UP and 393 DEG DOWN ( Table S2 ). As control, we used 1,169 gene loci expressed in DE but unaffected (padj>0.9) by OTX2 depletion. We found that >80% of DEG DOWN , >75% of DEG UP but <50% of unaffected loci had at least one OTX2 binding site ( Fig.S4F ). OTX2-bound DEGs, on average, showed a significantly higher number of OTX2 peaks than OTX2-bound unaffected genes ( Fig.S4G ). While OTX2 binding was enriched at both DEG DOWN and DEG UP , OTX2 peaks at DEG DOWN were stronger ( Fig.S4H ) and we observed a pronounced enrichment of DEG DOWN in the vicinity of the strongest OTX2 peaks (4 th quartile; Q4) ( Fig.4F ). These observations suggest that activating versus repressing functions of OTX2 may be determined by its target cis-regulatory elements. To gain insights into how OTX2 controls gene activation, we examined its binding kinetics around DEG DOWN . Despite the low abundance of peaks in the primed 1 and 2 categories (combined 26.2%) among all DE-associated OTX2 peaks ( Fig.4A ), more than half of DEG DOWN acquired OTX2 binding before DE specification ( Fig.4G ). These loci included regulators of cell adhesion/migration and antagonists of canonical WNT signaling that were already OTX2 bound in EpiSCs ( Emb , Cdh1 , Robo1 , Dkk1 ) or acquired binding in PS ( Sema6d , Amot , Shisa2 , Sfrp5 ) ( Fig.4G ). Loci of several DE/gut tube-associated TFs regulated by OTX2, such as Sox17 , Gata4 , Hesx1 , and Isl1, also acquired OTX2 binding in PS ( Fig.4G and Table S3 ). Most loci bound by OTX2 in EpiSCs and/or PS acquired additional peaks upon differentiation ( Fig.4D , E , and Fig.S4I ), demonstrating progressive transcriptional and chromatin remodeling. Genes with only de novo peaks included transcriptional, signaling, and migration regulators associated with later stages of gut tube development and patterning ( Tnnt1 , Has2 , Cxxc4 , Rfx6 ) ( Fig.4G ). DEG DOWN bound in EpiSCs (“primed 1”) were expressed at elevated levels in EpiSCs and PS before becoming further upregulated during DE specification ( Fig.4H ). Together, these observations are consistent with the notion that OTX2 binding in the epiblast prepares transcriptional programs required for gastrulation and germ layer formation. However, full activation of OTX2-controlled genes requires expansion of the OTX2 cistrome upon receipt of DE-specifying signals. OTX2 controls the activity of a subset of gene regulatory elements in DE The activation status of cis-regulatory elements (CREs), including enhancers and promoters, is a critical determinant of gene expression. To determine whether OTX2 controls CRE activity during DE formation, we first determined the genome-wide distribution of H3K27ac, a mark of active enhancers and promoters 44 using CUT&Tag 45 . H3K27ac distribution clearly distinguished EpiSCs, PS, and DE ( Fig.S5A ) with four major categories of largely promoter-distal peaks emerging: EpiSC-specific (K1), EpiSC/PS-shared (K2), PS-specific (K3), and DE-specific (K4) ( Fig.5A and Fig.S5B ). OTX2 only bound to few (<5%) candidate CREs active in EpiSCs (K1) ( Fig.5B ). This fraction was slightly higher (5–10%) for PS-associated CREs (K2, K3), before strongly increasing to about 40% of DE-associated CREs (K4) ( Fig.5B ). Accordingly, OTX2 motifs were enriched at K4 sites, while CREs in EpiSC and PS were dominated by motifs for TFs such as OCT4 (K1) or EOMES and the WNT co-factors TCF7L1/2 (K3) ( Fig.S5C ). Assessment of H3K27ac levels 21h after OTX2 depletion revealed a pronounced drop at promoter-distal, OTX2-bound sites normally active in DE (K4) ( Fig.5C ). In contrast, promoter-distal sites not bound by OTX2 and promoter-proximal sites (independent of OTX2 binding) showed little change in H3K27ac levels ( Fig.5C ). This indicates that OTX2 is required to establish or maintain the activity of a subset of DE-associated enhancers. In contrast, OTX2-bound K2 and K3 (PS-associated) CREs showed increased H3K27ac levels at the DE stage in absence of OTX2, suggesting failed repression ( Fig.5C ). Genome-wide analysis confirmed that OTX2 loss in DE results in several thousand differential peaks (log2FC>1: padj<0.1) that are less (n=1,614) or more (n=1,051) active ( Fig.5D ). In contrast, acute OTX2 depletion had little impact on H3K27ac levels in EpiSCs or PS ( Fig.5D ). Cell type-specific changes in CRE activity were consistent with the magnitude of cell type-specific transcriptional change ( Fig.5E ), with DEGs identified by bulk RNA-seq in DE (log2FC>1; padj<0.01, Table S4 ) showing strong correlation with DEGs identified by scRNA-seq ( Fig.S5E ). Acute OTX2 loss also resulted in reduced levels of H3K4me1, a developmentally controlled histone mark associated with poised and active enhancers 44 at several hundred sites in PS and DE ( Fig.S5F - G ), suggesting a role of OTX2 in enhancer priming in addition to activation. We observed almost no sites with increased H3K4me1 upon OTX2 depletion, suggesting that H3K27ac gain in OTX2-depleted DE occurs at sites already poised ( Fig.S5G ). OTX2 also binds to genomic regions not marked by H3K27ac or H3K4me1, suggesting possible additional chromatin-related functions of this TF ( Fig.S5H ). Together, our observations demonstrate that acute OTX2 loss affects gene expression in DE by altering the activity of OTX2-bound candidate distal enhancer elements. Opposing functions of OTX2 in enhancer regulation during DE specification We analyzed all genomic regions with significantly altered H3K27ac levels in DE upon OTX2 depletion at PS to better characterize the role of OTX2 in controlling CRE activity. This revealed three categories of sites ( Fig.6A and Table S5 ), which predominantly (>85%) localized to promoter-distal regions ( Fig.S6A ). K DE _1 sites (n=2,092) gained strong H3K27ac signal during differentiation, but this stage-specific activation failed in the absence of OTX2 ( Fig.6A , B and Fig.S6B ); K DE _2 sites (n=1,137) showed low baseline H3K27ac but were ectopically activated in OTX2-depleted DE ( Fig.6A , C and Fig.S6C ); K DE _3 sites (n=547) showed high H3K27ac levels in PS and failed to be effectively decommissioned in DE in the absence of OTX2 ( Fig.6A and Fig.S6C ). H3K4me1 levels at these sites showed similar, albeit less pronounced trends ( Fig.S6D ) while chromatin accessibility was reduced at K DE _1 sites upon OTX2 depletion ( Fig.S6E ). These findings document that OTX2 impacts chromatin features linked to CRE activity, consistent with roles in both activation and repression of enhancers in DE. H3K27ac changes matched the expression changes of nearby genes during development or upon OTX2 depletion ( Fig.6D ). Accordingly, genes near differential H3K27ac peaks were associated with similar processes as DEGs identified by RNA-seq ( Fig.S6F ). Closer inspection of genes transcriptionally affected by OTX2 loss frequently confirmed the presence of differential peaks, with DEG DOWN strongly enriched for K DE _1 peaks and DEG UP enriched for K DE _2 and K DE _3 peaks ( Fig.6E ), supporting a regulatory function of these sites. Genes whose expression and CRE activity depended on OTX2 included DE-associated TFs ( Sox17 , Rfx6 ), anterior gut markers ( Isl1 , Tnnt1 ) and adhesion/migration regulators ( Cxcr4 , Cdh1 , Emb , Sema6d ) ( Fig.6A , B and Fig.S6B ). In contrast, genes whose expression and CRE activity was stimulated by OTX2 depletion included TFs associated with non-DE-lineages ( Tfap2c , Prdm6 ) and regulators/targets of canonical WNT signaling ( Axin2 , Lgr5 , Wnt3 , Sulf2 )( Fig.6A , B ). We also observed significantly elevated H3K27ac levels upon OTX2 depletion in the vicinity of the Eomes and Foxa2 loci encoding important gastrulation/mesendoderm regulators ( Fig.S6C ), although the degree of transcriptional upregulation of these loci measured by bulk RNA-seq – while statistically significant – did not pass our foldchange benchmark (log2FC 0.87; padj 2.01E-34 for Foxa2 and log2FC 0.24; padj 0.013 for Eomes ) ( Table S4 ). Consistent with a direct role of OTX2 in controlling chromatin activity and transcriptional output, most K DE _1 peaks were OTX2 bound in DE with an overrepresentation of strong (Q4) peaks ( Fig.6F and Fig.S6G ). Of note, close to 40% (501 of 1,342) of OTX2-bound K DE _1 peaks exhibited OTX2 preoccupancy in EpiSCs and/or PS ( Fig.6G ), supporting a molecular priming function of OTX2 binding. OTX2-bound K DE _1 sites had higher H3K27ac levels and were more DE-restricted compared to CREs not affected by OTX2 loss ( Fig.S6H ), suggesting OTX2 regulates a subset of strong, tissue-specific enhancers. In contrast, K DE _2/3 peaks bound by OTX2 were not characterized by strong binding ( Fig.6F and Fig.S6G ) and showed only weak enrichment for the OTX2 consensus motif compared to K DE _1 sites ( Fig.6H ). Of note, >65% (137 or 207) of OTX2-bound K DE _3 peaks showed OTX2 preoccupancy ( Fig.6G ), suggesting that OTX2 binding to these CREs in EpiSCs and/or PS might facilitate their later decommissioning. Consistent with elevated canonical WNT activity upon OTX2 depletion, the motifs of TCF7L1/2 and LEF1 were enriched at K DE _2/3 sites ( Fig.6H ). We also observed enrichment of the motif of EOMES ( Fig.6H ), which has critical functions in mesoderm in addition to endoderm 38 , 46 . To determine how the regulatory functions of OTX2 and EOMES intersect, we integrated published EOMES genome occupancy and genomics data from EOMES KO DE-like cells 47 . This revealed EOMES binding to K DE _1 peaks and reduced H3K27ac and ATAC-seq signal at these regions in absence of EOMES ( Fig.S6J , K ), albeit less pronounced than upon OTX2 depletion. In addition, EOMES showed stronger binding than OTX2 to K DE _2/3 sites ( Fig.S6I ), which – in contrast to the increased H3K27ac observed upon OTX2 depletion ( Fig.6A ) – showed reduced activity ( Fig.S6J ) and accessibility ( Fig.S6K ) in EOMES KO DE. In agreement with these chromatin changes, we observed that DE-associated genes such as Sox17 , Dkk1 and Cxcr4 were downregulated upon loss of EOMES or OTX2, while PS/mesoderm-associated genes upregulated in DE upon OTX2 depletion, including Mixl1 , Prdm6 , Rspo3 , and Tfap2c, were downregulated in cells lacking EOMES ( Fig.6I and Table S5 ). These findings are consistent with OTX2 synergizing with EOMES to activate aspects of endoderm-associated gene expression while antagonizing mesoderm-associated gene expression driven by EOMES. OTX2 is required for faithful specification of human DE OTX2 has a high degree (99.7%) of amino acid sequence conservation between mouse and human 48 ( Fig.S7A ), suggesting evolutionary pressure to preserve protein function. To determine the role of OTX2 during human DE development, we established OTX2-dTAG lines ( Fig.7A ) from MEL-1 and H9 human embryonic stem cells (hESCs), two well-characterized lines with established DE potential 49 , 50 . The human degron allele allowed rapid (1h) and efficient (>90%) OTX2 depletion ( Fig.7B ), using the degrader dTAG-v1 51 which was more effective in human cells than dTAG-13 ( Fig.S7B ). We differentiated OTX2-dTAG hESCs towards DE with two slightly different protocols – one using identical signaling manipulations to the one applied in mouse (“Protocol 1”) 33 , the other applying additional PI3K inhibition during PS specification (“Protocol 2”) 10 ( Fig.S7C ). In accordance with the developmental resemblance of human pluripotent cells to the post-implantation epiblast, we observed widespread OTX2 expression in OTX2-dTAG hESCs ( Fig.S7D ). Like in mouse, OTX2 levels increased during DE formation ( Fig.S7E ), suggesting similarities in the control of the OTX2 locus in both species. Differentiation of OTX2-dTAG hESCs towards endoderm in the presence of dTAG-v1 resulted in significantly reduced numbers of CXCR4 + CD117 + cells ( Fig.7C , D ). Analysis by qPCR of select gene loci representing pathways and processes affected by OTX2 depletion in mouse DE revealed evidence for dysregulation of similar developmental modules. Thus, we observed altered expression of developmental TFs, including lower levels of the DE-associated TF HESX1 and elevated levels of “ectopic” TFs such as MIXL1 and TFAP2C in human DE specified in the absence of OTX2 ( Fig.7E ). In contrast, SOX17 levels were not downregulated at RNA or protein level in human DE upon OTX2 depletion ( Fig.7E and Fig.S7F ), suggesting differences in the regulation of this locus between mouse and human (see Fig.1J and Fig.2D ). While levels of the OTX2-EGFP reporter were minimally affected by OTX2 depletion ( Fig.S7G ), fluorescence microscopy showed that EGFP + human DE lacking OTX2 protein failed to form the dense cell clusters that were abundant in endodermal cultures expressing this TF ( Fig.7F , G ). This observation suggests a deficit in the ability of OTX2-deficient DE to self-organize, which is reminiscent of experiments with mouse foregut (see Fig.3D and S3H ). Together, these observations are consistent with the notion that OTX2 is a regulator of human gut tube specification and suggest that some of its gene regulatory functions are conserved between mice and humans ( Fig.7H ).
discussion: Discussion By combining scalable in vitro models of embryonic lineage specification with genetic tools for controlled protein degradation, we have identified a previously unappreciated role of OTX2 in DE specification and patterning. In contrast to TFs such as SOX17 32 or EOMES 38 whose loss in KO mouse models results in entire parts of the early gut tube missing, OTX2 depletion in our system does not cause a complete loss of endoderm identity or the generation of non-viable cells. Instead, loss of OTX2 during or after DE formation leads to gene expression and chromatin changes consistent with dysregulation of canonical WNT signaling, altered expression of migratory and cell adhesion molecules, and ectopic expression of regulators of other early embryonic lineages. These abnormalities prevent timely exit from PS and the faithful establishment of anterior endoderm identity, providing a molecular explanation for the mislocalization of mesendodermal cells and impaired foregut formation in OTX2-deficient mice 17 , 21 , 22 . While we found no role for OTX2 in supporting paraxial mesoderm development, other studies have suggested OTX2 depletion favors germline over mesoderm differentiation 24 . Therefore, potential functions of OTX2 in somatic lineages beyond DE and NE require further investigation. We found that OTX2 binds to candidate enhancers of loci encoding regulators of cellular signaling, cell adhesion, and gut tube development, and establishes and maintains chromatin accessibility and histone acetylation. OTX2 binds a significant number of its DE target loci already in EpiSCs and/or PS, preceding their transcriptional upregulation. However, acute OTX2 depletion in EpiSCs and PS has limited impact on H3K27ac levels or transcription in these cells. While the molecular function of the early binding to DE-associated loci remains to be determined, our observations suggest OTX2 pre-positioning during gastrulation, as reported for other TFs in different developmental contexts 52 . The extensive de novo OTX2 binding in DE might be driven by the availability of specific co-factors and/or the occupation of low-affinity binding sites enabled by elevated OTX2 levels. In addition to the activation of DE-associated enhancers, OTX2 is involved in the timely decommissioning of some PS-associated enhancers and to prevent the activation of enhancers of “ectopic” embryonic lineages. Of note, ~30% of OTX2-repressed enhancers are OTX2-bound in DE, suggesting direct repression. In agreement with the notion that OTX2 can exert both activating and repressive functions depending on the nature of the CREs it binds and the co-factors it engages with 53 , we observed weak binding of OTX2 to enhancers it represses and poor enrichment of the OTX2 consensus motif. The de-repression of enhancers not bound by OTX2 might be due to the elevated activity of canonical WNT signaling and/or of other TFs, such as EOMES, which we found required for the expression of mesoderm-associated gene loci repressed by OTX2. High levels of Otx2 expression are maintained in the anterior foregut and possibly in thyroid and thymus progenitors 5 , raising the possibility that OTX2 functions also at later stages of gut tube development. The similar Otx2 / OTX2 expression kinetics during the early stages of mouse and human in vitro development, the enrichment of the OTX2 motif at mesendodermal enhancers during hESC differentiation 54 , and our analysis of gene expression changes in OTX2-depleted human DE, suggests partial conservation of OTX2 function during mammalian evolution. Furthermore, the observation that some of the OTX2 targets we identified in DE are shared with other lineages, including Dkk1 in the anterior visceral endoderm 55 and Hesx1 in the forebrain 56 , supports the existence of a shared molecular blueprint of anterior-posterior patterning that employs OTX2. The general notion of OTX2 being a germ layer rather than a pluripotency regulator is consistent with the survival of OTX2 KO mice until early organogenesis 21 and the mild and delayed defects in mouse pluripotent stem cell lines upon OTX2 deletion 30 , 57 . In summary, our studies provide insight into the transcriptional regulation of early gut tube patterning by OTX2 and suggest similarities in the molecular control of anteriorization across tissues and species. The integration of multipurpose degron alleles into efficient directed differentiation regimens of pluripotent cells represents a tractable and readily generalizable platform to dissect these similarities further and to study other developmental processes in mammals.
